---
title: "Introduction"
---

::: {.callout-note collapse=false}
## 🎯 Learning Objectives

- Develop a clear understanding of the definition and historical evolution of robotics
- Identify and explain the fundamental components of robots, including links and joints
- Analyze different robot kinematic structures, such as serial, parallel, and hybrid configurations
- Examine the diverse applications of robotics across domains including industry, space, surgery, exoskeleton systems, exploration, and humanoid robotics
:::

## Definition and History of Robotics

### What Defines a Robot?

The definition of a robot has shifted from early mythological "automata" to sophisticated autonomous agents. To maintain academic and industrial consistency, we rely on the international standard:

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
> "An **actuated mechanism** programmable in two or more axes with a degree of autonomy, moving within its environment, to perform intended tasks."
>
> ISO 8373:2021
:::

This definition highlights that a robot is not merely a machine, but a system capable of **Sensing** its surroundings, **Processing** that information, and **Acting** physically upon the world.

### Historical Milestones

The history of robotics is a timeline of increasing environmental complexity—moving from the predictable factory floor to the unpredictable terrain of Mars and, eventually, the human household. @fig-robot-history provides an interactive look at these turning points.

```{python}
#| label: fig-robot-history
#| fig-cap: "Evolution of Robotics: From Unimate to Humanoids."
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd

# --- TIMELINE DATA ---
data = [
    (1950, "Unimate", "The first industrial robot, automating heavy lifting at GM.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Unimate"),
    (1966, "Shakey", "The first mobile robot to perceive and reason about its surroundings.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shakey_the_robot"),
    (1997, "Sojourner", "First wheeled vehicle to roam another planet (Mars).", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sojourner_(rover)"),
    (2011, "Curiosity", "Mars rover exploring Gale Crater with a complex scientific payload.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curiosity_(rover)"),
    (2021, "Optimus", "Tesla's general-purpose bipedal humanoid robot project.", "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Optimus_(robot)"),
    (2024, "Figure 01", "AI-first humanoid utilizing vision-language-action models.", "https://robotsguide.com/robots/figure")
]

df = pd.DataFrame(data, columns=["Year", "Event", "Description", "Link"])
df['y'] = [1, -1] * (len(df)//2) + ([1] if len(df)%2 else [])

# --- COLORS (Obsidian/Midnight Theme) ---
BG_MAIN      = "#0f172a"  
BG_CARD      = "#1e293b"  
ACCENT_CYAN  = "#38bdf8"  
TEXT_DIM     = "#94a3b8"  
BORDER_COLOR = "#334155"  

fig = go.Figure()

# 1. Timeline Base Line
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
    x=[df['Year'].min()-5, df['Year'].max()+5], y=[0, 0],
    mode='lines', line=dict(color=BORDER_COLOR, width=2),
    hoverinfo='none'
))

for i, row in df.iterrows():
    # 2. Vertical Stems
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[row['Year'], row['Year']], y=[0, row['y']],
        mode='lines', line=dict(color=BORDER_COLOR, width=1),
        hoverinfo='none'
    ))
    
    # 3. Nodes (Circles)
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=[row['Year']], y=[0],
        mode='markers',
        marker=dict(size=12, color=ACCENT_CYAN, line=dict(width=4, color=BG_MAIN)),
        hovertext=f"<b>{row['Event']}</b><br>{row['Description']}",
        hoverinfo="text" 
    ))

    # 4. Styled Rounded Boxes (Simulated via high borderpad)
    label_text = (
        f"<span style='color: {TEXT_DIM}; font-size: 11px;'>{row['Year']}</span><br>"
        f"<b><a href='{row['Link']}' target='_blank' "
        f"style='color: white; text-decoration: none;'>{row['Event']}</a></b>"
    )
    
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=row['Year'], y=row['y'] * 1.6, # Slightly more offset for rounded look
        text=label_text,
        showarrow=False,
        font=dict(size=13),
        align="center",
        bgcolor=BG_CARD, 
        bordercolor=ACCENT_CYAN, # Using cyan for a subtle "glow" border
        borderwidth=1.5,
        borderpad=12,            # HIGH padding creates the rounded/capsule effect
        opacity=1
    )

# --- LAYOUT ---
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False, fixedrange=True),
    yaxis=dict(showgrid=False, zeroline=False, showticklabels=False, range=[-4, 4], fixedrange=True),
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    hoverlabel=dict(
        bgcolor=BG_CARD,
        font_size=13,
        font_color="white",
        bordercolor=ACCENT_CYAN,
        # Rounds the tooltip box as well
        namelength=-1
    ),
    margin=dict(l=10, r=10, t=10, b=10),
    showlegend=False,
    width=740,
    height=450
)

fig.show()
```

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

# --- CONFIGURATION ---
LINK_LENGTH = 8
AXIS_LENGTH = 2
CANVAS_RANGE_X = [-8, 8]
CANVAS_RANGE_Y = [-1.5, 8.5]
WIDTH = 740
HEIGHT = 400 

# --- COLOR PALETTE ---
AXIS_X_COLOR = '#e63946'   
AXIS_Y_COLOR = '#2a9d8f'   
LINK_COLOR   = '#7547ff'   
JOINT_BASE   = '#0052a4'   
JOINT_TIP    = '#ff7300'   
JOINT_OUTLINE= '#ff7300'   
ARC_COLOR    = '#ecec42'   
TEXT_COLOR   = '#ffffff'   
ACCENT_COLOR = '#60a5fa'   

def create_link_traces(theta_deg):
    theta_rad = np.radians(theta_deg)
    x_e, y_e = LINK_LENGTH * np.cos(theta_rad), LINK_LENGTH * np.sin(theta_rad)
    
    ax_x = go.Scatter(
        x=[0, AXIS_LENGTH], y=[0, 0],
        mode='lines+text',
        name="X-Axis",
        line=dict(color=AXIS_X_COLOR, width=2),
        text=["", "X"],
        textposition="top right",
        textfont=dict(color=TEXT_COLOR)
    )

    ax_y = go.Scatter(
        x=[0, 0], y=[0, AXIS_LENGTH],
        mode='lines+text',
        name="Y-Axis",
        line=dict(color=AXIS_Y_COLOR, width=2),
        text=["", "Y"],
        textposition="top right",
        textfont=dict(color=TEXT_COLOR)
    )

    link = go.Scatter(
        x=[0, x_e], y=[0, y_e],
        mode='lines',
        line=dict(width=12, color=LINK_COLOR),
        name="Link"
    )

    base_joint = go.Scatter(
        x=[0], y=[0],
        mode='markers',
        marker=dict(
            size=24,
            color=JOINT_BASE,
            line=dict(width=3, color=JOINT_OUTLINE)
        ),
        name="Base"
    )

    tip_joint = go.Scatter(
        x=[x_e], y=[y_e],
        mode='markers',
        marker=dict(
            size=18,
            color=JOINT_TIP,
            line=dict(width=2, color=JOINT_OUTLINE)
        ),
        name="Tip"
    )

    # Motion Arc
    t = np.linspace(0, theta_rad, 40)
    arc = go.Scatter(
        x=1.8 * np.cos(t),
        y=1.8 * np.sin(t),
        mode='lines',
        name="Joint angle",
        line=dict(color=ARC_COLOR, width=2.5, dash='dot')
    )

    return [arc, link,ax_x, ax_y, base_joint, tip_joint]


def get_layout_settings(angles):
    sliders = [dict(
        active=0,
        currentvalue={
            "prefix": "θ: ",
            "suffix": "°",
            "font": {"size": 20, "color": ARC_COLOR},
            "offset": 20
        },
        tickcolor=TEXT_COLOR,
        font={"color": TEXT_COLOR},
        pad={"t": 10},
        steps=[dict(
            method="animate",
            label=str(a),
            args=[[str(a)], {"frame": {"duration": 0, "redraw": False}, "mode": "immediate"}]
        ) for a in angles]
    )]

    return dict(
        xaxis=dict(range=CANVAS_RANGE_X, visible=False, fixedrange=True),
        yaxis=dict(range=CANVAS_RANGE_Y, visible=False, fixedrange=True,
                   scaleanchor="x", scaleratio=1),
        sliders=sliders,
        paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
        plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
        showlegend=False,
        width=WIDTH,
        height=HEIGHT,
        margin=dict(l=20, r=20, t=20, b=20),
        annotations=[dict(
            x=0, y=0,
            text="R",
            showarrow=False,
            font=dict(color=TEXT_COLOR, size=20)
        )]
    )


def build_revolute_viz():
    fig = go.Figure()
    angles = np.arange(0, 181, 2)
    
    # Initialize
    fig.add_traces(create_link_traces(angles[0]))
    
    # Frames
    fig.frames = [
        go.Frame(data=create_link_traces(a), name=str(a))
        for a in angles
    ]
    
    # Layout
    fig.update_layout(**get_layout_settings(angles))
    
    return fig


# Display
fig = build_revolute_viz()
fig.show()
```
